articles.title: Building Homes for Bats: A Costly Project
articles.summary: In Gloucestershire, England, a big road project is happening near the M5 motorway. As part of this, two special houses are being built for bats because the road work might disturb their homes. These bat houses cost £166,000, which is a lot of money. Some people, like councillors Julian Tooke and Vernon Smith, are worried about the high cost. They think the money could be used for other important things like schools or libraries. However, bats are protected by law, so the council has to find a new place for them to live. The council got four quotes to make sure they were paying a fair price. The new road junction should be finished by 2029.

articles.title: A Little Hard Play Keeps You Healthy
articles.summary: Doing just a few minutes of hard exercise each day can help keep you from getting sick. Scientists studied nearly 96,000 people and found that short bursts of activity, like running fast or playing hard, lower the risk of diseases like arthritis, heart problems, and dementia. These activities make you breathe harder and help your body fight sickness better. You don't need to go to the gym; you can do things like taking the stairs quickly or walking fast. Even a little bit of this hard play each week makes a big difference for your health.

articles.title: Gloves Can Mess Up Science About Tiny Plastic Bits
articles.summary: Scientists found that the gloves they wear in labs might make it look like there are more tiny plastic pieces in the environment than there really are. These gloves, made from materials like nitrile and latex, can leave small particles on tools used to test air and water samples. The particles are called stearates, which are not plastic but look like plastic in tests. This can cause mistakes, making scientists think they found microplastics when they didn't. Researchers from the University of Michigan suggest using special cleanroom gloves that don't leave as many particles. They say microplastics are still a big problem, but we need to be careful with our tests to get the right numbers.

articles.title: Sperm Get Lost in Space Without Gravity
articles.summary: Scientists found that sperm have trouble finding their way in space-like conditions where there is no gravity. They used a special machine to make it feel like space and tested sperm from humans and other animals. The sperm could not navigate through a maze that looks like the female body parts where babies start. This means gravity helps sperm swim to the egg. Adding a hormone called progesterone helped the sperm do better in space-like conditions. Also, when eggs were fertilized in these conditions, fewer babies started to grow, and they grew slower. This research shows that having babies in space might be hard, and we need to learn more to help.

articles.title: A Giant Volcano Under the Sea is Waking Up
articles.summary: Scientists found that a huge volcano under the sea in Japan is filling up with hot, melted rock called magma again. This volcano, called Kikai caldera, erupted 7,300 years ago in the biggest explosion of our current time. Using special tools like airguns and seismometers in the ocean, researchers took pictures of the Earth below to see the magma. They discovered that new magma is flowing into the volcano, not old magma from the last eruption. This helps scientists learn how giant volcanoes like Yellowstone might behave in the future. Understanding this can help predict when big eruptions might happen to keep people safe.

articles.title: A Special Place Gets Money to Make It Better
articles.summary: A beautiful area in southern England called the North Wessex Downs might get £1 million to make it nicer. Wiltshire Council is helping to ask for this money from the National Grid, a company that manages electricity. The money could be used to add more gates that are easy to open, help farmers grow plants in a way that is good for animals, plant new hedges and trees, and fix walking paths. The National Grid gives money to projects that make electricity pylons look less ugly. They plan to ask for six grants in 2026. People like Paul Sample and Jemima Sellwood are excited because this will help wildlife and trees grow better in the area.
